summary: I was born in Virginia, and my early connection to nature really comes from my grandfather, who was a country preacher in Tidewater, Virginia. We spent a lot of time outdoors together, canoeing and exploring the woods and water. That experience planted the seed for my lifelong pull toward the natural world.

I grew up in Louisville, Kentucky, which was a very urban and suburban environment compared to my grandfather's rural Virginia. Despite the city life, my family background and my grandfather's influence gave me a deep appreciation for nature. Louisville was where I lived before I fully embraced my environmental writing and activism.

I studied at the University of Kentucky, where I had the privilege of learning from Guy Davenport, my main mentor. I earned my undergraduate and master's degrees there, and eventually became a faculty member after a long, non-traditional academic journey. UK was also where I connected with many colleagues and began my environmental writing and activism.

Guy Davenport was my main mentor at the University of Kentucky, and he taught me a lot about writing, especially how to shape sentences carefully. He was a heavy smoker and sadly died of lung cancer, but his body was donated to the Markey Cancer Center. His influence on my writing was profound, and his passing coincided with meeting Wendell Berry, which was a turning point for me.

The Markey Cancer Center in Kentucky received Guy Davenport's body after he died of lung cancer. We dedicated a tree to him at the Arboretum there, which was a meaningful tribute. It was also around this time that I met Wendell Berry, marking a significant moment in my career.

I met Wendell Berry at the Markey Cancer Center dedication, and he became a mentor and collaborator. He was organizing a tour for writers to fly over coal fields and talk to affected people, inspired by John Dos Passos's poverty tours. Working with Wendell and others was a powerful experience that helped me engage deeply with mountaintop removal mining issues.

John Dos Passos inspired Wendell Berry's idea to organize a tour for writers to observe coal mining impacts firsthand, similar to his poverty tours in the 1930s. This historical reference shaped our approach to environmental storytelling and advocacy. It was a model for connecting urban readers with Appalachian realities.

Harper's Magazine was where I first published 'Death of a Mountain,' an article about mountaintop removal mining. Initially, an editor there suggested I focus on one mountain for a clearer story, which led me to write about Lost Mountain. Harper's provided a platform that helped bring attention to the coal industry's environmental impacts.

Mountaintop removal mining is the environmental issue I focused on most, especially in Eastern Kentucky. I wrote extensively about how it destroys landscapes, contaminates water, and affects communities. Our writing and activism put the coal industry on the defensive, even as they tried to dismiss us as overly emotional.

Teri Blanton was one of the great people I met during the fight against mountaintop removal mining. She was part of the activist and writer community that I found myself surrounded by during that time. Being with people like Teri made the work feel like being part of a supportive herd, which helped me stay engaged despite my discomfort with crowds.

Dave Cooper was another important figure I met in the environmental activism circle focused on coal mining issues. Like Teri Blanton, he was part of the group that helped me feel connected and supported during our efforts. These relationships were crucial as I navigated my role as a writer-activist.

Dwight Billings was a key academic ally who encouraged me to seek tenure protection after I wrote critical op-eds about the University of Kentucky's stance on coal. He helped get the ball rolling in my department, which eventually led to my promotion to assistant professor. Dwight was part of a network of Kentucky and Appalachian scholars who supported my work.

Lost Mountain became the focus of my book and environmental writing because it was a perfect metaphor for the destruction caused by mountaintop removal mining. I found the name while researching mining permits at the Office of Surface Mining. The mountain was small and was destroyed within a year, making it a powerful symbol for readers unfamiliar with the issue.

Patsy Carter is a person I wrote about in my book Lost Mountain; her daughter was tragically killed by an overloaded coal truck. Her story represents the human cost of coal mining in Appalachia. Including her experience helped me convey the real-life consequences of environmental destruction to urban readers.

As mentioned, Harper's Magazine was the outlet where I published my early work on mountaintop removal mining, including 'Death of a Mountain.' The editorial guidance I received there helped shape my approach to storytelling by focusing on a single mountain. Harper's played a crucial role in amplifying the environmental issues I was passionate about.

The Office of Surface Mining was where I went to research mining permits, which are dense and full of legal jargon. It was there that I discovered the permit for Lost Mountain, which became central to my writing. This research was foundational in grounding my environmental stories in concrete, official documentation.

Julia Butterfly Hill is an activist I admire who famously sat in a tree for two years to stop logging. I heard her speak once, and she gave advice that really stuck with me: 'find your tree.' That inspired me to find my own focus in environmental activism, which became mountaintop removal mining and Lost Mountain.

Robinson Forest is a place where I took students to inspire them about nature writing. Although surrounded by strip mining, I wanted them to experience what E.O. Wilson called the naturalist trance. The constant blasting from mining made it hard, but it also deepened our awareness and writing about environmental issues.

Eastern Kentucky is where I took long walks to observe the impact of coal mining firsthand. It was a place that deeply influenced my environmental writing and activism. The region's struggles with mountaintop removal mining became the focus of much of my work.

I mentioned Donald Trump as an example of someone who might destroy what they love, contrasting him with most people who protect what they care about. It was part of a reflection on human nature and environmental stewardship. The comparison helped me articulate why I believe most people want to protect the natural world.

E.O. Wilson inspired me with his concept of the naturalist trance, which I tried to share with my students during trips to Robinson Forest. His work helped me think deeply about our connection to nature and the importance of environmental writing. Wilson's ideas shaped my teaching and my approach to nature writing.

The Kingdom of God, as described in the Gospels, became a theological framework for me to understand and value the natural world. My grandfather was a fundamentalist preacher, but I saw the Kingdom of God as something present in the natural world around us. This belief motivated me to protect the environment as a sacred responsibility.

Spinoza was mentioned in the broader context of environmental and philosophical thought, though I didn't elaborate much on him. His ideas about nature and existence resonate with some of the themes I explore in my writing. Philosophers like Spinoza help frame the deeper questions about our relationship to the natural world.

The Gospels influenced my understanding of the Kingdom of God as something present in the natural world, not just a distant spiritual realm. This interpretation shaped my environmental ethic and motivated my writing and activism. Bringing religious arguments into coal rallies often unsettled opponents, showing the power of faith in environmental debates.
